children.text: *This post contains affiliate links. If you sign up through them, we may earn a commission at no extra cost to you.*
folk CRM Keeps Getting Better — And Here's What Just Dropped

If you've been following my coverage of relationship-first CRMs, you know I have a soft spot for tools that respect how humans actually work. folk CRM has been on my radar since it first launched, and every update they ship makes me more convinced this is the CRM most teams actually need.

The latest batch of features? Genuinely exciting. Not the "we added 47 settings no one asked for" kind of update. Real, tangible improvements that change daily workflows. folk CRM recently earned 4 out of 5 efficiency points from editors at Efficient App, praised specifically for ease of learning, workflow presence, team adoption, and AI assistance. That's not hype — that's users confirming what the folk team has been building toward.
Before I dive into specifics, if you're curious and want to try folk CRM right now,
check out their plans and start free here
. The free tier is generous enough to actually test whether it fits your team.
What's New in folk CRM: The Features That Changed My Workflow
MCP Integration

This is the headline feature. folk CRM now supports MCP (Model Context Protocol), which opens up the tool to a new generation of AI-powered workflows. In plain terms: folk can now connect with external AI tools and data sources in ways that weren't possible before.
For teams already experimenting with AI assistants outside folk, this integration means your CRM becomes a hub rather than a silo. Data flows in and out more fluidly. If you're exploring what
AI-powered workflow tools can do
, this MCP update puts folk in a category that very few CRMs occupy right now.
AI Assistants That Actually Learn Your Context

folk's AI assistants aren't generic chatbots bolted onto a database. They learn from *your* data — your contact history, your deal notes, your communication patterns. The result is automation that feels intelligent rather than random.
Here's what this looks like in practice:
Drafting follow-up emails
that reference previous conversations and match your team's tone
Summarizing long email threads
so you can catch up on a contact relationship in 30 seconds instead of 10 minutes
Suggesting next actions
based on where a deal sits in your pipeline and historical patterns
Enriching contact profiles
automatically by pulling in publicly available data
The AI doesn't replace your judgment. It handles the repetitive thinking so you can focus on the conversations that matter. For anyone building a
step-by-step CRM workflow
, this AI layer cuts setup and maintenance time dramatically.
Magic Fields

This is folk CRM's secret weapon, and the newest iteration is even better. Magic fields let you create custom data fields that auto-populate based on available information. Think of them as smart columns in your contact database.
Say you're tracking investor contacts. A magic field might automatically pull in:
Their LinkedIn headline
Company size
Recent funding rounds
Mutual connections

You define what context matters for your workflow, and folk fills in what it can find. For recruiters, this might mean candidate location and skill sets. For agency owners, client industry and social media presence. The fields adapt to your use case rather than forcing you into someone else's template.
The Core Features That Made folk Stand Out in the First Place
Pipeline Management That Doesn't Overwhelm

folk's pipeline view is clean. No unnecessary columns, no confusing stage names you didn't choose. You build pipelines that match how your team actually works — whether that's a simple three-stage sales funnel or a complex multi-team recruitment process.

Dragging deals between stages feels natural. Filtering by owner, value, or custom tags takes two clicks. If you've ever stared at a cluttered Salesforce dashboard wondering where your warm leads went, folk's approach will feel like breathing fresh air.
Email Campaigns Built In
Running email campaigns from your CRM used to mean paying for a separate tool or dealing with clunky integrations. folk includes email campaigns as a native feature. You can:
Segment your contacts by tags, pipeline stage, or custom fields
Personalize templates using contact data and magic fields
Track opens and clicks directly inside folk
See campaign performance alongside your deal pipeline

For small teams running outreach without a dedicated marketing tool, this eliminates at least one subscription from your stack. Combined with folk's contact enrichment, your campaigns get sharper over time as the system learns more about your audience.
Team Collaboration Without the Chaos

folk was built for teams, not just individuals. You can assign contacts to specific team members, leave notes on profiles visible to everyone (or just certain people), and share pipelines across departments. The activity feed shows what's happening across your entire contact universe without requiring anyone to file status reports.
This collaborative layer is where folk really shines compared to traditional CRMs that treat team features as an afterthought. If you want to understand why teams choose folk over alternatives,
this breakdown of folk CRM's advantages
covers the comparison in detail.
Who Is folk CRM Ideal For?
folk isn't trying to be Salesforce. It's not trying to be HubSpot. It's trying to be the CRM you actually enjoy opening every morning. Here's who gets the most value from it:
Investment teams and VCs

— Managing hundreds of relationships across portfolio companies, LPs, and deal flow. folk's magic fields pull in founder backgrounds, funding history, and mutual connections automatically. The pipeline view keeps deal stages crystal clear across a team.
Recruiters and talent teams
— Tracking candidates through hiring funnels. Custom pipelines per role, automated enrichment pulling in LinkedIn data, and shared team visibility so no candidate falls through the cracks.
Agencies and consultancies
— Managing client relationships, tracking proposals, and running outreach campaigns all from one place. The
use cases for folk CRM
in agency settings are particularly compelling.
Small sales teams
— folk's pricing starts at $24/member/month for the Standard plan (annual billing), making it accessible for teams of 3-10 people who need real pipeline management without enterprise complexity.
See their current pricing and start your trial here
Founders and solopreneurs
— Even solo operators managing partnerships, press contacts, or investor relations find folk's interface clean enough to use daily without feeling overwhelmed.

The common thread? folk works best for people who think in relationships, not just transactions. If your business depends on knowing who you talked to, what you discussed, and what comes next — folk was built for exactly that.
Getting Started: My Recommendation

I've tested a lot of CRMs. Most of them collect digital dust after the initial setup because the daily experience feels like filing taxes. folk is different. The interface is fast, the learning curve is gentle, and the AI features give you a reason to keep coming back.
Here's what I'd suggest:
Start with the free plan
and import a small contact list (50-100 people you actually talk to)
Set up one pipeline
for your most active workflow — sales deals, recruiting, or partnerships
Enable magic fields
and let folk enrich your contacts automatically
Try drafting one follow-up email
using the AI assistant and see how it handles your context
Invite one teammate
and test the collaboration features before rolling out to your whole team
Teams that follow this incremental approach tend to adopt folk fully within two weeks. That's unheard of in the CRM world, where 90-day implementation timelines are the norm for larger platforms.
For practical advice on getting the most from the tool,
these folk CRM tips and best practices
cover workflows that experienced users swear by.
Why folk CRM Deserves Your Attention in 2025
The CRM market is crowded. Every week there's a new tool promising to revolutionize how you manage contacts. Most of them are variations on the same spreadsheet-in-a-fancy-interface approach.

folk actually built something different. The combination of AI that learns your context, magic fields that adapt to your workflow, MCP integration that connects to the broader AI ecosystem, and a team collaboration layer that doesn't require a manual to understand — that's a genuinely distinct product.

At $24/member/month on the Standard plan, folk competes on price with tools that offer significantly less. The free plan lets you test everything with real data before committing. And the team ships updates frequently enough that the product keeps improving while you're using it.
If you're tired of CRMs that feel like homework,
give folk CRM a try
. I think you'll be surprised how quickly it becomes part of your daily rhythm.

FAQ
Q: Is folk CRM free?

A: Yes, folk offers a free plan with core features including contact management, basic pipeline views, and limited AI assistance. The Standard plan at $24/member/month (billed annually) unlocks full AI features, email campaigns, advanced magic fields, and MCP integration. You can
start with the free plan here
and upgrade when you're ready.
Q: How does folk CRM compare to traditional CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot?

A: folk is designed for teams that prioritize relationship management over complex sales automation. It's lighter, faster to set up, and more affordable than enterprise CRMs. Teams typically achieve full adoption in under two weeks, compared to months with larger platforms. folk's AI-native approach and magic fields also provide a more modern experience than traditional CRM architectures.
Q: Can I import my existing contacts into folk CRM?

A: Absolutely. folk supports CSV imports, LinkedIn integration, and connections with tools like Gmail and Outlook. Magic fields can automatically enrich imported contacts with additional data like company information, social profiles, and recent activity. Most users have their full contact database migrated and enriched within an hour.
